_provenance.note: Hand-authored for CE-PACK-NEUTRAL-DEFAULT — the neutral DEFAULT pack's ontology. A domain-neutral content-review domain: auditing a generated response against the source material it claims to be grounded in. Internally consistent with taxonomy_snapshot.json — every gradeable flag's code is in a tier; each Tier-1 code has the same owning role as the snapshot's tier1_owners; the snapshot lenses are a subset of the tier union. Declares NO verification_contracts so the grade path never reaches an external grounding service (the minimal standalone cut). The prose is needle-free (no HIPAA/patient/medication/dosage/allergy/clinical/SOAP/escalation/consent/transcript wording) so render_role_questions emits a clean non-clinical judge prompt.
_provenance.seeded_by: hand-authored (the core default pack has no external seed source)

flags.definition: The response asserts a fact, figure, rule, or entitlement that does not appear in the provided source material and was not established earlier in the exchange.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The claim is supported by the source material or was already established earlier in the exchange. The response faithfully paraphrases a real source statement without changing its meaning.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The response cites a figure or rule that the source material does not contain. 2) The response invents an entitlement with no basis in the provided material.

flags.definition: The response makes a strong or definitive assertion the available material does not support, presenting a guess or inference as established fact.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The assertion is backed by the source material, or is clearly framed as an inference or estimate rather than an established fact.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The response states an outcome as guaranteed when the material gives no basis for it. 2) The response presents a speculative conclusion as if it were confirmed.

flags.definition: The response states something that directly contradicts the provided source material or an earlier point in the exchange.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The response corrects an earlier mistake and clearly says so. The apparent contradiction is a reasonable clarification rather than a reversal of the source.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The source says one thing and the response asserts the opposite. 2) An earlier point is reversed by the response without acknowledgement.

flags.definition: The response omits material context the situation requires the reader to know in order to act correctly on it.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: No additional context is required for the situation, or the required context is already present in the response or earlier in the exchange.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The response states a conclusion but omits a caveat the material requires. 2) The response gives a step but omits a precondition the reader must satisfy first.

flags.definition: The response contradicts itself — two statements within the same response cannot both be true.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The two statements are about different things, or one clearly supersedes the other with an explicit correction.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The response gives one figure in one sentence and a different figure for the same quantity in the next. 2) The response both affirms and denies the same point.

flags.definition: The response violates a stated style or formatting requirement for the output.
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The response meets the stated style requirements, or no style requirement was given.
flags.when_to_use: 1) A required format (a list, a heading, a length bound) is not followed. 2) The response adopts a register the instructions explicitly disallowed.

flags.definition: A numeric or categorical value in the note does not match the value the source transcript supports (a dose, lab value, vital, or measurement drift).
flags.when_NOT_to_use: The value is simply absent (that is an omission, not a mismatch), or the difference is a rounding the source explicitly supports.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The note states a dose/lab/vital that differs from the transcript. 2) A value is charted with a changed unit or magnitude the source does not license.

flags.definition: The note asserts a specific clinical detail (a finding, symptom, or descriptor) that the transcript never establishes.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The note adds an exam finding or symptom not in the source. 2) A descriptor is invented beyond what the transcript supports.

flags.definition: The note fails to surface a red-flag or escalation the transcript indicates is warranted.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The source signals a red-flag symptom the note does not escalate. 2) A required follow-up/referral is omitted.

flags.definition: History Omission — clinical defect (already in the shared taxonomy; added to this agent's gradeable ontology).
flags.when_to_use: See the shared taxonomy lens for this code.

flags.definition: Dissent Erasure — clinical defect (already in the shared taxonomy; added to this agent's gradeable ontology).
flags.when_to_use: See the shared taxonomy lens for this code.

flags.definition: Intent Erasure — clinical defect (already in the shared taxonomy; added to this agent's gradeable ontology).
flags.when_to_use: See the shared taxonomy lens for this code.

flags.definition: Proxy Misattribution — clinical defect (already in the shared taxonomy; added to this agent's gradeable ontology).
flags.when_to_use: See the shared taxonomy lens for this code.

flags.definition: Wrong Dosage — clinical defect (already in the shared taxonomy; added to this agent's gradeable ontology).
flags.when_to_use: See the shared taxonomy lens for this code.

flags.definition: The note documents a diagnosis MORE SPECIFIC than the record supports (e.g. records "Dementia" but the note charts "Alzheimers disease") — unsupported specificity / upcoding.
flags.when_to_use: 1) The note names a specific subtype when the record only supports the general condition. 2) The note adds diagnostic specificity the transcript does not license.

questions.text: Does every fact, figure, or rule the response cites appear in the provided source material or earlier in the exchange?
Does the response include the context the situation requires the reader to know?
Does the response avoid presenting guesses or inferences as established facts the material does not support?
Is the response internally consistent — do its statements all hold together?
Does anything in the response contradict the provided source material or an earlier point in the exchange?
Does the response follow any stated style or formatting requirement?

params.arguments_jute:
$let:
  codes: $ resource.case.subsumption_codes
  recipe: $ resource.case.injection_recipes.0
$body:
  record_code: $ codes.record_snomed
  note_code: $ codes.note_snomed
  record_term: $ recipe.pre_value
  note_term: $ recipe.post_value

verification_contracts.question: SNOMED terminology battery (validity/mislabel/category/is-a direction): does live SNOMED support the note diagnosis against the record diagnosis, or is it invalid, mislabelled, mis-categorised, or an upcode?

params.fact: the patient explicitly refused the tetanus vaccine because of a past bad reaction

verification_contracts.question: Is the patient's tetanus-vaccine refusal preserved in the note?

params.fact: the patient stated an intent to restart their antiretroviral (HIV) medication

verification_contracts.question: Is the patient's stated intent to restart antiretroviral therapy preserved?

params.fact: the patient's long occupational history working at a state psychiatric facility

verification_contracts.question: Is the patient's occupational history preserved?

params.statement: the patient is currently taking their seizure medication

verification_contracts.question: Was the medication statement said by the patient or the guardian?
